title: Pastry cream
subtitle: The custard filling for eclairs, tarts, and Danish pastries
excerpt: Pastry cream is a thick, egg-yolk custard cooked with flour or cornflour until it holds a shape when cold. It is the standard filling for eclairs, fruit tarts, and Danish pastries. Made properly, it is smooth and glossy and sets firmly enough to pipe without running. It keeps in the fridge for 3 days, covered tightly.
sourceNotes: Crème pâtissière is a French classical preparation documented in patisserie manuals from the eighteenth century onward. The formula is: egg yolks, sugar, flour or cornflour, and milk. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, gives an early British version. The method described here follows the standard French technique.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 500 ml (enough to fill 12 eclairs or one 23 cm tart
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead. Press cling film directly onto the surface of the cream before refrigerating to prevent a skin forming.

content.text: Pastry cream is a thick, set custard made by cooking egg yolks, sugar, and cornflour with hot milk until the mixture thickens into a smooth, pipeable cream. The cornflour is what makes it set firm rather than pour; the constant whisking while it boils prevents lumps and cooks out the floury taste. Once cold, it holds its shape and slices cleanly.

items.prepNote: or one vanilla pod, split and scraped
cold, cubed; stirred in off the heat for gloss

content.text: Heat the milk with the vanilla in a medium saucepan over medium heat until just steaming, not boiling. If using a vanilla pod, add it to the milk while it heats, then remove before proceeding.
In a bowl, whisk the egg yolks and sugar together until pale and slightly thick. Whisk in the cornflour until fully incorporated.

Pour a quarter of the hot milk over the yolk mixture, whisking constantly. Pour this mixture back into the saucepan with the remaining milk, and return to medium heat. Whisk continuously) do not stop; as the cream heats and thickens. Once it begins to bubble, whisk hard for 1 full minute; the starch needs to boil to cook out the floury taste. The finished cream should be thick, glossy, and fall from the whisk in heavy ribbons.

Off the heat, stir in the cold butter if using. Pass through a sieve into a clean bowl for a perfectly smooth result. Press cling film directly onto the surface of the cream and refrigerate until completely cold, at least 1 hour. Before using, beat briefly with a whisk or spatula to restore smoothness.
